answer:
**In the Falsafa corpus edition of *Manusmṛti*, the specific rules on *madhuparka* hospitality appear in Chapter 4 (verses labeled Mn_3.119–120), in the broader section on household duties following marriage rites (traditional Chapter 3 continues into this). Chapter 3 proper (verses Mn_3.1–66) covers student vows, marriage rules, and family conduct but does not mention *madhuparka*.**[^1][^2]

The text states that the following guests merit *madhuparka* (a mixture of honey, curd, etc., as a respectful offering):

- A king (*rājan*), sacrificial priest (*ṛtvij*), *snātaka* (Vedic graduate), *guru*, beloved father-in-law (*śvaśura*), or maternal uncle (*mātula*)—**when they arrive after a full year has passed**.[^1]

- Separately, **a king or a learned Brahmana (*Brahmaṇa vidvaṃs*), but only if present at a sacrificial rite (*yajñe sthite); not otherwise**.[^2]

This fits within rules for honoring *atithi* (unexpected guests, typically Brahmin wanderers) via the five great sacrifices, prioritizing Vedic scholars while distinguishing ordinary visitors.[^3]

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 4, p-c1f760](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-c1f760).

[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 4, p-0c0be7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-0c0be7).

[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 4](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/).
